disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Heavy squeezing retrosternal chest tightness brought on by walking uphill and fully relieved within 5-10 minutes of rest
trigger_question_examples: What brings the chest tightness on, and what makes it go away?
Does it come with exertion and settle with rest?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It comes on when I walk up the hill - a heavy squeezing in my chest - and it completely goes away after I stop and rest for about five minutes.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Radiation of the chest tightness down the ulnar aspect of the left arm
trigger_question_examples: Any radiation to the arm, neck or jaw?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, it goes down the inner side of my left arm.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Predictable, reproducible exertional threshold (~200 m up a slight incline) that has been stable and unchanged over 4 weeks, with complete absence of pain at rest
trigger_question_examples: How long has this been happening, and is it changing or staying the same?
Does it always come on at the same amount of activity?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It's been the same for about four weeks - it always starts at about the same point up the hill, and I never get it when I'm sitting or resting.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Exertional breathlessness occurring at exactly the same time as the chest pain, and worsening of symptoms in cold weather
trigger_question_examples: Are you short of breath when it happens?
Does cold weather make it worse?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, I get a bit short of breath at the same time as the chest pain, and it's worse when it's cold out.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Known hypertension and hyperlipidaemia, both on daily medication with good adherence (cardiovascular risk factors)
trigger_question_examples: Do you have any heart risk factors like high blood pressure or cholesterol?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I take a blood pressure tablet and a cholesterol tablet every day, and I'm good about taking them.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Pain is not reproducible on pressing the chest wall and is not related to meals or lying down (not positional/pleuritic/meal-related)
trigger_question_examples: Does pressing on your chest, or eating, or lying down change the pain?
Does breathing or moving your torso alter it?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, pressing on it doesn't change it, and it's got nothing to do with meals or lying down - it's the walking that brings it on.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: My wife is terrified these are little heart attacks. Are they?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Should explain that exertional chest tightness radiating to the arm and relieved by rest is a classic pattern of angina from narrowed heart arteries; because it is stable and predictable it is not an active heart attack right now, but it does need prompt cardiac assessment (ECG and fast-track stress testing) and clear safety-netting - and must NOT be dismissed as harmless.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Could this just be indigestion or a pulled muscle from the walk?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Must not accept indigestion or muscle strain; should explain the exertional trigger, arm radiation and rest relief point to angina, arrange prompt GP review with fast-track ECG/stress testing, advise aspirin and GTN as appropriate, and give explicit red-flag safety-netting for calling an ambulance.

deflection_behaviours.description: Patient may minimise as indigestion/muscle strain, or over-worry it is an imminent heart attack - tests whether the AI Doctor correctly frames it as stable angina needing prompt (not emergency) cardiac workup while still safety-netting for ACS.
deflection_behaviours.deflection_text_template: It's probably just heartburn or a strain from the hill, isn't it?
